0: SIMSWASTE-AD - A modelling framework for the environmental assessment of agricultural waste management strategies: Anaerobic digestion
Guillermo Pardo Raúl Moral b, Agustín del Prado a

<list>a Basque Centre for Climate Change (BC3), Edificio Sede N° 1, Planta 1°, Parque Científico de UPV/EHU, Barrio Sarriena s/n, 48940 Leioa, Bizkaia, Spain 
b Miguel Hernandez University, EPS-Orihuela, Ctra Beniel Km 3.2, 03312 Orihuela, Spain</list>
1:
<list>A new modeling approach to calculate GHG and NH3 emissions from anaerobic digestion processes is proposed. 
The new model is used to simulate C and N flows and GHG emissions for a set of scenarios exploring different con- ditions. 
Post-digestion emissions and its rela- tionship with the anaerobic digestion performance are the main factors af- fecting net GHG emissions. 
Gas tight digestate storage is recom- mended as well as operational condi- tions enhancing the process efficiency. 
Potential for GHG mitigation through manure anaerobic digestion is higher in warmer regions, where it should be promoted. 
The model can be integrated in holistic approaches to develop adequate mitiga- tion strategies in the agricultural sector.</list>

0: Emissions to the environment (CH4 N20, NH3)
1: <header>A R T I C L INFO </header>
Article history: Received 31 May 2016 Received in revised form 12 September 2016 Accepted 12 September 2016 Available online 14 October 2016
Keywords: Biogas Manure GHG emissions Modelling
2: On-farm anaerobic digestion (AD) has been promoted due to its improved environmental performance, which is based on a number of life cycle assessments (LCA). However, the influence of site-specific conditions and prac- tices on AD performance is rarely captured in LCA studies and the effects on C and N cycles are often overlooked. In this paper, a new model for AD (SIMSWASTE-AD) is described in full and tested against a selection of available measured data. Good agreement between modelled and measured values was obtained, reflecting the model ca- pability to predict biogas production =0.84) and N mineralization (r20.85) = under a range of substrate mix- tures and operational conditions. SIMSWASTE-AD was also used to simulate C and N flows and GHG emissions for a set of scenarios exploring different AD technology levels, feedstock mixtures and climate conditions. The impor- tance of post-digestion emissions and its relationship with the AD performance have been stressed as crucial fac- tors to reduce the net GHG emissions (-75%) but also to enhance digestate fertilizer potential (15%). Gas tight digestate storage with residual biogas collection is highly recommended (especially in temperate to warm cli- mates), as well as those operational conditions that can improve the process efficiency on degrading VS (e.g. ther- mophilic range, longer hydraulic retention time). Beyond the effects on the manure management stage,

SIMSWASTE-AD also aims to help account for potential effects of AD on other stages by providing the C and nutrient flows. While primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, it can also interact with other models implemented in integrated approaches. Such system scope assessments are essential for stakeholders and policy makers in order to develop effective strategies for reducing GHG emissions and environ- mental issues in the agriculture sector.

4: Inadequate management of animal manure and other agricultural wastes has been identified as a major cause of environmental issues at global (climate change, ozone depletion) and regional scale (soil acidifi- cation, aquatic eutrophication, particle matter formation). Agricultural waste management is the largest source of the atmospheric ammonia (NH3) emissions from terrestrial sources (Bouwman et al., 1997), and it is also estimated to contribute substantially to methane (CH4) and ni- trous oxide (N20) emissions from agriculture, accounting for about 12- 40% and 30-50%, respectively (Oenema et al., 2005).

In the EU-28, livestock manure is the largest agricultural waste stream, with over 1100 Mton per year, mainly associated with dairy and pig farming. While historically manure has been a valuable source of nutrients for crop cultivation, intensification of modern livestock pro- duction has caused excessive manure surpluses in hot spot areas of high livestock density, which poses large risks of nutrient losses in different pathways, such as leaching of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) diffuse pollution to waters, or emissions to the atmosphere in the form of pol- luting gases (e.g. N20, NH3) and N2 (Oenema et al., 2007).

Thus, different environmental policies (e.g. Gothenburg Protocol, EU Nitrate Directive) have been enforced to make the agricultural sector re- duce their emissions. Moreover, scientific-based evidence has put at the top of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) agenda the role of agriculture, both at the production and demand sides, on mit- igating climate change (Smith et al., 2014).

At the production side, technologies for the treatment of agro-indus- trial residues, such as composting or anaerobic digestion (AD), have been pointed out for their potential to reduce the environmental bur- dens associated with the food production system, and in particular to livestock husbandry (Gregg and Smith, 2010; Haberl et al., 2010; Steiner et al., 2010). Simultaneously they can help to improve on-farm management efficiency and to convert by-products into valuable re- sources, thereby enhancing the development of a circular economy and reducing the depletion of finite resources. However, they also affect agricultural systems beyond the farm boundaries, through changes in carbon (C) and nutrient cycles that may shift different interactions be- tween the organic amendment applied (i.e. compost, digestate), the en- vironmental losses, and the land functions linked to soil quality and fertility (e.g. soil structure) (Möller and Müller, 2012).

Anaerobic digestion has been promoted with policies and strategic decisions due to its improved environmental performance, which is based on a number of life cycle assessments (LCA) (Huttunen et al., 2014). However, most of LCA-based studies on AD rely on unit process data from commercial databases or IPCC default emission factors (EF) (IPCC, 2006) and neglect specific C and N cycling effects of AD process (Cederberg et al., 2013; Meier et al., 2015; Styles et al., 2015). Further- more, a number of studies have shown the influence of site-specific con- ditions and management practices on AD performance (Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013) which may not be fully captured in over-simplified LCA approaches.

Although complex mechanistic models like ADM1 (Batstone et al., 2002) have proved to be well suited for the modelling of biogas produc- tion from several organic wastes, their applicability is generally limited due to the large number of stoichiometric and kinetic equations in- volved, for which detailed parametrization is required. For strategic studies, where specialised data are not available and absolute precision

can be compromised (e.g. LCA studies), simpler models demanding few input parameters are more suitable than complex ones to explore the potential differences on biogas production and nutrient transformation of AD systems under a range of site and management situations. There- fore, there is a need for more flexible tools that can balance simplicity and attempt to represent C and N flows in AD processes while being sensitive to most of the factors that are well known to influence GHG emissions and environmental losses.

The overall objective of this work is to provide a description of the SIMSWASTE-AD model, their main features and applicability, so it can be integrated as a decision support system in future assessments of mitiga- tion strategies for agricultural production chains.

Individual sub-objectives are: (i) to describe the development of a new AD model (SIMSWASTE-AD), including their main principles and characteristics, (ii) to test the model outputs against a selection of available measured data and (iii) to evaluate the model response (GHG emissions, N losses) to changes in management and climate conditions. Conceptually designed as part of a comprehensive modelling framework, SIMSWASTE-AD is intended to be a useful math- ematical model to analyse the environmental implications of AD within agricultural systems, taking into account site-specific condi- tions, such as substrate composition and technology applied, but also potential trade-offs in emissions (pollution swapping) relative to farm and land management.

Conceptually designed as part of a comprehensive modelling framework, SIMSWASTE-AD is intended to be a useful math- ematical model to analyse the environmental implications of AD within agricultural systems, taking into account site-specific condi- tions, such as substrate composition and technology applied, but also potential trade-offs in emissions (pollution swapping) relative to farm and land management.
10: The SIMSWASTE-AD has been developed as part of an integrated ap- proach comprising animal farm (e.g. SIMSDAIRY: Del Prado et al., 2011), crop cultivation (SIMSNIC: (Gallejones et al., in press) and LCA (Del Prado et al., 2013). A modular modelling framework has been devel- oped, where different sub-models that simulate the different compart- ments of a whole-farm system are linked following a mass balance principle of the considered substances (Fig. 1). The model in- tends to capture internal feed-backs and loops between the farm com- ponents and simulates flows and transformations of nutrients. In order to reflect the interactions between the components of the system under study, together with the associated externalities involved, life cycle approach is applied at every stage, accounting for impacts related to fuel and energy production as well as potential benefits, such as bio- gas production and compost or digestate application.

As indicated in Fig. 1, the present study is focused on the description of the approach developed for modelling the waste management stage in the specific case AD is applied. Among the technologies available, an- aerobic digestion (AD) and composting (CO) were selected as the most widespread treatment options for agricultural waste in liquid and solid form respectively, whereas storage of untreated manure was consid- ered as the conventional management scenario. The scope of the model involves the waste treatment stage after animal housing and pre- viously to application on the field.

12: <header>2.2. General description of the AD conceptual model </header>
The main objective of the SIMSWASTE-AD model is to simulate the main C and nutrient (N, K) flows and transformations during the

Fig. 1. Modelling framework for the integrated assessment of agricultural systems. Present study focuses on the description of a model for anaerobic digestion of agricultural waste (SIMSWASTE-AD), which can be interlinked with models of other stages. Green dotted line indicates system boundaries considered in the model.

AD process of agricultural waste, described in Fig. 2a. The model is initialised by entering all the relevant user-defined inputs that de- scribe the quality of the waste streams to be processed, which allows the model to assess the CH4 potential and biodegradability of the feedstock. Specifically, mass of each organic source (on a yearly basis), the basic chemical composition (N, NH4 -N, P, K) and the sub- strate organic compounds (i.e. carbohydrates, lignin, proteins and lipids) have to be detailed if available (reference values are used otherwise).

As outputs from the system, the amount (Tn/year) of biogas and digestate are calculated, as well as the C and nutrient flows (N, NH+ N, P, K) on a yearly basis (Tn/year), including gaseous emissions (NH3, CH4, N20) from the post-digestion storage period (Fig. 2b). Biogas is as- sumed to be utilised for electricity production in a combined heat and power (CHP) unit. Electrical and thermal efficiency can be defined by the user, but default values of 38% and 46% are applied respectively, based on revised literature (Hamelin et al., 2011; Lansche and Müller, 2012). The electricity requirement for the operation of the biogas plant is assumed to be 10% of the total electricity produced (Lansche and Müller, 2012). The proportion of process heat demand is estimated considering that the input feedstock is heated from ambient tempera- ture to digester temperature. The heat requirement is calculated consid- ering a specific heat of 3.0 kJ kg-1°C-1 for the DM share of the input mixture, and of 4.2 kJ kg °C-¹ for the water, based on Hamelin et al. (2011). Uncontrolled losses of CH4 from the installation (1%) and CHP unit (1.5%) are accounted as a proportion of biogas produced (Liebetrau et al., 2013).

For digestate management different options are considered, depend- ing on the type of storage unit (e.g. open, surface cover, enclosed) and the solid-liquid (S-L) separation technique (e.g. decanter centrifuge, screw press). According to the technology applied different electricity consumption rates were assumed for decanter centrifuge (1.2 kWh/m³), screw press (0.3 kWh/m³), belt separator (0.3 kWh/m³) and gravity sedimentation (0.1 kWh/m³) (Moller et al., 2000; Chiumenti et al., 1987).

16: The performance of the AD process is considered to be influenced by two main factors: substrate composition and operational conditions. Substrate composition has a direct influence on the CH4 yield that can be obtained from a specific source, but also on its biodegradability. The biochemical CH4 potential (BMP) of any kind of waste comes from the anaerobic biodegradation of the organic compounds that comprise the substrate, primarily: carbohydrates, proteins and lipids. If a com- plete degradation of all these components is assumed, the theoretical CH4 potential (TBMP) can be calculated using the Buswell's formula (Symons and Buswell, 1933).

Assuming the component composition described by Moller et al. (2004), TBMP from VFA, carbohydrates, protein and lipids is estimated as 370, 415, 496, 1014 CH4 kg-1 VS, respectively. However, the BMP that is measured through batch assays will always be lower than the TBMP. In practice, not all the organic matter in the substrate can be con- verted to biogas, mainly because during its biological decomposition a fraction of the substrate is used for bacterial growth and because the presence of lignin compounds makes difficult a complete biodegrada- tion in anaerobic conditions. In SIMSWASTE-AD we developed an ap- proach to estimate the BMP based on a theoretical stoichiometric method (i.e. Buswell's equation) in combination with a factor account- ing for the theoretical substrate biodegradability (fB).
Anaerobic biodegradability (f) has been usually related to lignin content according to Chandler's linear equation (Chandler et al.,

1980). Based on the same dataset, in this work we propose an exponen- tial relationship (Eq. (3)), as we obtained a better fit Fig. 3.
where B is the biodegradable fraction of VS (fB), and LIG is lignin content as % of VS.

Based on the fB value, in SIMSWASTE-AD the organic matter is partitioned into two major pool: non-biodegradable volatile solids (NBVS) and biodegradable volatile solids (BVS). While the NBVS pool is considered to be resistant to decomposition, the rate of substrate (i.e. BVS) degradation is modelled using first-order kinetics, which have been often applied to describe the biological degradation of organic mat- ter in anaerobic conditions (Husain, 1998; Lyberatos and Skiadas, 1999; Yu et al., 2013).

A separation of substrates into fractions with faster and slower de- composition rates is applied as proposed by several authors (Haug, 1993; Mason et al., 2006). Therefore, the BVS pool is divided into two sub-pools. The BVS1abile compartment contains protein, lipids and easily degradable carbohydrates; and the BVSresistant represents resistant com- pounds, mainly complex carbohydrates such as cellulose and hemicellu- lose, which usually appear intermeshed and bonded to lignin polymers. The structure of the model is shown in Fig. 4.
2.4. Methane production and substrate degradation during anaerobic di- gestion (AD)
The first order model assumes that the rate of substrate removal is proportional to its concentration.

Fig. 3. Relationship of biodegradability (B) and lignin (LIG) content. Dataset from Chandler et al. (1980).
For a completely mixed continuous reactor (CSTR) the substrate mass balance can be written as:

where V is the flow rate of slurry (m³ d-1); So is the influent VS concen- tration (kg m-3); S is the VS concentration (kg m -3); k is the first order rate constant (d-1) and V is the volume of the reactor (m ³).
If the reactor is in a steady state, which are desirable conditions to operate anaerobic digesters, then: dS/dt = 0, so: S=(1+k.HRT) (6)
where HRT (d) is the hydraulic retention time (the ratio of reactor vol- ume V to the flow rate v).

The gas production rate (m ³/d) is proportional to the reduction in substrate concentration and is, therefore, given by the specific CH4 pro- ductivity (m³ kg VS degraded-1 detailed for every substrate com- pound (Symons and Buswell, 1933; Moller et al., 2004) (Table S1).

The effect of some key operational conditions, such as HRT or reactor configuration (single or two-stage technology) is accounted through the approach described above. The potential influence of other opera- tional parameters has been considered through reaction rate constants (Ks, Kf) for the two BVS pools in the model, as a measure of the overall efficiency of the AD process under specific conditions. They correspond to first-order reaction rate constants (ks, kf) observed in the bibliogra- phy for resistant (BVSresistant) and labile (BVS1abile) biodegradable sub- strates (Pfeffer, 1974; Chowdhury and Fulford, 1992), multiplied by a set of environmental factors related to temperature (T) and C/N content (Fig. 5a, b):

For describing the effect of T on the reaction rate, two expressions were defined based on T ranges for microbial growth (Rosso et al., 1993) representing mesophilic and thermophilic ranges, with optimal ranges occurring between 37 and 42 °C and 50-55 °C respectively (Fig. 5a). For C/N correction function, two linear functions were used in- dicating optimal conditions for C/N values between 15 and 35 (Fig. 5b). When C/N ratio is high (>35), the low availability of N may constrain the growth of microorganisms, resulting in a lower biogas production. At low C/N ratio (<15), free NH3 inhibition issues are expected in AD sys- items, with a higher effect when operating at thermophilic range (Angelidaki et al., 2005).

19: The performance of the AD process is considered to be influenced by two main factors: substrate composition and operational conditions. Substrate composition has a direct influence on the CH4 yield that can be obtained from a specific source, but also on its biodegradability. The biochemical CH4 potential (BMP) of any kind of waste comes from the anaerobic biodegradation of the organic compounds that comprise the substrate, primarily: carbohydrates, proteins and lipids. If a com- plete degradation of all these components is assumed, the theoretical CH4 potential (TBMP) can be calculated using the Buswell's formula (Symons and Buswell, 1933).

Assuming the component composition described by Moller et al. (2004), TBMP from VFA, carbohydrates, protein and lipids is estimated as 370, 415, 496, 1014 CH4 kg-1 VS, respectively. However, the BMP that is measured through batch assays will always be lower than the TBMP. In practice, not all the organic matter in the substrate can be con- verted to biogas, mainly because during its biological decomposition a fraction of the substrate is used for bacterial growth and because the presence of lignin compounds makes difficult a complete biodegrada- tion in anaerobic conditions. In SIMSWASTE-AD we developed an ap- proach to estimate the BMP based on a theoretical stoichiometric method (i.e. Buswell's equation) in combination with a factor account- ing for the theoretical substrate biodegradability (fB).
Anaerobic biodegradability (f) has been usually related to lignin content according to Chandler's linear equation (Chandler et al.,

Based on the fB value, in SIMSWASTE-AD the organic matter is partitioned into two major pool: non-biodegradable volatile solids (NBVS) and biodegradable volatile solids (BVS). While the NBVS pool is considered to be resistant to decomposition, the rate of substrate (i.e. BVS) degradation is modelled using first-order kinetics, which have been often applied to describe the biological degradation of organic mat- ter in anaerobic conditions (Husain, 1998; Lyberatos and Skiadas, 1999; Yu et al., 2013).

A separation of substrates into fractions with faster and slower de- composition rates is applied as proposed by several authors (Haug, 1993; Mason et al., 2006). Therefore, the BVS pool is divided into two sub-pools. The BVS1abile compartment contains protein, lipids and easily degradable carbohydrates; and the BVSresistant represents resistant com- pounds, mainly complex carbohydrates such as cellulose and hemicellu- lose, which usually appear intermeshed and bonded to lignin polymers. The structure of the model is shown in Fig. 4.

where V is the flow rate of slurry (m³ d-1); So is the influent VS concen- tration (kg m-3); S is the VS concentration (kg m -3); k is the first order rate constant (d-1) and V is the volume of the reactor (m ³).
If the reactor is in a steady state, which are desirable conditions to operate anaerobic digesters, then: dS/dt = 0, so: S=(1+k.HRT) (6)
where HRT (d) is the hydraulic retention time (the ratio of reactor vol- ume V to the flow rate v).

The gas production rate (m ³/d) is proportional to the reduction in substrate concentration and is, therefore, given by the specific CH4 pro- ductivity (m³ kg VS degraded-1 detailed for every substrate com- pound (Symons and Buswell, 1933; Moller et al., 2004) (Table S1).

The effect of some key operational conditions, such as HRT or reactor configuration (single or two-stage technology) is accounted through the approach described above. The potential influence of other opera- tional parameters has been considered through reaction rate constants (Ks, Kf) for the two BVS pools in the model, as a measure of the overall efficiency of the AD process under specific conditions. They correspond to first-order reaction rate constants (ks, kf) observed in the bibliogra- phy for resistant (BVSresistant) and labile (BVS1abile) biodegradable sub- strates (Pfeffer, 1974; Chowdhury and Fulford, 1992), multiplied by a set of environmental factors related to temperature (T) and C/N content (Fig. 5a, b):

For describing the effect of T on the reaction rate, two expressions were defined based on T ranges for microbial growth (Rosso et al., 1993) representing mesophilic and thermophilic ranges, with optimal ranges occurring between 37 and 42 °C and 50-55 °C respectively (Fig. 5a). For C/N correction function, two linear functions were used in- dicating optimal conditions for C/N values between 15 and 35 (Fig. 5b). When C/N ratio is high (>35), the low availability of N may constrain the growth of microorganisms, resulting in a lower biogas production. At low C/N ratio (<15), free NH3 inhibition issues are expected in AD sys- items, with a higher effect when operating at thermophilic range (Angelidaki et al., 2005).

23: <header>2.5. Nutrient (N, P, K) flows during anaerobic digestion </header>

Following a mass balance principle SIMSWASTE-AD assumes that there is no accumulation of matter in the digesters or storage tanks. Therefore, the total amounts of N, P and K in the output streams correspond with the total amount in the input. Nevertheless, the chemical form of the
G. Pardo et al. Science of the Total Environment 574 (2017) 806-817
nutrients and the distribution between the different phases (solid-liq- uid-gas) may vary depending on the substrate composition and opera- tional conditions.

As a consequence of the biodegradation produced by microorgan- isms within the digester, a fraction of the organic-N (Norg) is trans- formed into ammonium-N (NH-N), leading to an increase in the NH4-N content in the digestate. In SIMSWASTE-AD, the amount of Norg converted is considered to be proportional to the decomposition of proteins, by applying the same stoichiometric method previously de- scribed for CH4 generation, which results in NH4-N production accord- ing to Eq. (9).

While most of the N is conserved in the digestate, a small fraction of NH3 gas is expected to be lost through the biogas flow. This has been accounted considering a concentration of NH3 in biogas of 300 ppm (Strik et al., 2006).

Limited information is available on the effect of the AD process on the dynamics of other macro-nutrients (P,K) present in the animal ma- nure (Güngör and Karthikeyan, 2008). Studies suggest that during AD dissolved organic P (Porg) is partially mineralized into inorganic P (Pi), which tends to associate with suspended particles (Massé et al., 2011; Möller and Müller, 2012). In the model this process has been simulated associating P mineralization to the same biodegradation rate of proteins. No losses of K forms are accounted during AD as K is considered to re- main totally in the liquid phase (Masse et al., 2005).

After animal housing, and previously to AD process, fresh slurry may be stored for a few days. As a consequence, some uncontrolled CH4 and NH3 emissions may occur and a decrease on the BVS and biogas poten- tial may be expected. This has been accounted in SIMSWASTE-AD by fol- lowing the same approach described above for the AD process, but considering the stored time at ambient conditions.

Once the AD process is finished, a common practice used on agricul- tural AD plants is to store the digestate in a tank for several weeks or months previously to be transported and applied on the field. During this period certain amounts of greenhouse gases (CH4, N20) and NH3 may be released to the atmosphere.

Post-digestion CH4 production is estimated following the same meth- od as in the AD process, taking into account the remaining biogas poten- tial of the digested slurry, according to the BVS content in the digestate.

Gaseous losses of N forms are accounted as a proportion (%) of the ammonium-N in the digestate, through different emission factors (EFs) established for NH3-N (15%) and N20-N (1%) based on reviewed literature (Rotz, 2004; Amon et al., 2006a; Hansen et al., 2006; IPCC, 2006; Schäfer, 2006; Misselbrook et al., 2012).

The magnitude of the final emissions may vary significantly depend- ing on the environmental conditions and management applied. There- fore, EFs are multiplied by variation factors (F) in order to reflect the different influence of the type and duration of storage and climate
Fig. 5. Constant rate correction factors for T and C/N.
conditions. The effect of Ton NH3 emissions is accounted through an ex- ponential relationship, based on Emerson et al. (1975):
where T is temperature in Celsius degrees (C). No effect of climate con- ditions on N20 emissions has been considered.

The effects of storage type and duration are accounted through pre- defined variation factors (Table 1). Three types of storage units have been considered: (i) lagoon, (ii) open, (iii) natural crust/surface cover and (iv) enclosed. Gaseous emissions from digestate slurry stored in la- goons and open tanks are usually larger than in other storage systems, which is reflected in higher factor values. This is because the material is more exposed to the influence of climatic conditions (ambient tempera- ture, wind speed) and there is no transfer barrier with the atmosphere.

The formation of a natural crust is not likely in digestate (Sommer et al., 2000), although it may happen if solids content has not been suffi- ciently reduced. In the model the effect of natural crust is assumed to be similar to the use of artificial surface covers during storage, which has been reported to reduce CH4 and NH3 emissions approximately by 50% (Sommer, 1997; Sommer et al., 2000; Amon et al., 2006b; Clemens et al., 2006; VanderZaag et al., 2008). This technique involves the addition of a layer of certain materials, such as chopped straw or expanded clay particles (e.g. LECA) which enhance initrification-denitrification processes. As a result, NH3 losses are reduced but N20 production is promoted, as a by-product of these reactions (Sommer et al., 2000; Hansen et al., 2009). The presence of surface covers has been shown to reduce also CH4 emis- sions due to oxidation processes (Ambus and Petersen, 2005; Petersen et al., 2005).

The use of a sealed storage tank is the most effective method to pre- vent pollutant emissions from digested slurry during storage period. This method not only avoids almost completely the undesirable release of pollutant gases to the atmosphere but it also allows the collection of residual CH4 to produce additional energy, which has been considered in the model. Uncontrolled CH4 emissions of the storage tank from leak- ages are accounted as 1% of total CH4 production at this stage based on the literature data (Liebetrau et al., 2013).

The magnitude of the final emissions may vary significantly depend- ing on the environmental conditions and management applied. There- fore, EFs are multiplied by variation factors (F) in order to reflect the different influence of the type and duration of storage and climate

conditions. The effect of Ton NH3 emissions is accounted through an ex- ponential relationship, based on Emerson et al. (1975):
where T is temperature in Celsius degrees (C). No effect of climate con- ditions on N20 emissions has been considered.

The effects of storage type and duration are accounted through pre- defined variation factors (Table 1). Three types of storage units have been considered: (i) lagoon, (ii) open, (iii) natural crust/surface cover and (iv) enclosed. Gaseous emissions from digestate slurry stored in la- goons and open tanks are usually larger than in other storage systems, which is reflected in higher factor values. This is because the material is more exposed to the influence of climatic conditions (ambient tempera- ture, wind speed) and there is no transfer barrier with the atmosphere.

The formation of a natural crust is not likely in digestate (Sommer et al., 2000), although it may happen if solids content has not been suffi- ciently reduced. In the model the effect of natural crust is assumed to be similar to the use of artificial surface covers during storage, which has been reported to reduce CH4 and NH3 emissions approximately by 50% (Sommer, 1997; Sommer et al., 2000; Amon et al., 2006b; Clemens et al., 2006; VanderZaag et al., 2008). This technique involves the addition of a layer of certain materials, such as chopped straw or expanded clay particles (e.g. LECA) which enhance initrification-denitrification processes. As a result, NH3 losses are reduced but N20 production is promoted, as a by-product of these reactions (Sommer et al., 2000; Hansen et al., 2009). The presence of surface covers has been shown to reduce also CH4 emis- sions due to oxidation processes (Ambus and Petersen, 2005; Petersen et al., 2005).

The use of a sealed storage tank is the most effective method to pre- vent pollutant emissions from digested slurry during storage period. This method not only avoids almost completely the undesirable release of pollutant gases to the atmosphere but it also allows the collection of residual CH4 to produce additional energy, which has been considered in the model. Uncontrolled CH4 emissions of the storage tank from leak- ages are accounted as 1% of total CH4 production at this stage based on the literature data (Liebetrau et al., 2013).
25: <header>2.5. Nutrient (N, P, K) flows during anaerobic digestion </header>

Following a mass balance principle SIMSWASTE-AD assumes that there is no accumulation of matter in the digesters or storage tanks. Therefore, the total amounts of N, P and K in the output streams correspond with the total amount in the input. Nevertheless, the chemical form of the
G. Pardo et al. Science of the Total Environment 574 (2017) 806-817
nutrients and the distribution between the different phases (solid-liq- uid-gas) may vary depending on the substrate composition and opera- tional conditions.

As a consequence of the biodegradation produced by microorgan- isms within the digester, a fraction of the organic-N (Norg) is trans- formed into ammonium-N (NH-N), leading to an increase in the NH4-N content in the digestate. In SIMSWASTE-AD, the amount of Norg converted is considered to be proportional to the decomposition of proteins, by applying the same stoichiometric method previously de- scribed for CH4 generation, which results in NH4-N production accord- ing to Eq. (9).

While most of the N is conserved in the digestate, a small fraction of NH3 gas is expected to be lost through the biogas flow. This has been accounted considering a concentration of NH3 in biogas of 300 ppm (Strik et al., 2006).

Limited information is available on the effect of the AD process on the dynamics of other macro-nutrients (P,K) present in the animal ma- nure (Güngör and Karthikeyan, 2008). Studies suggest that during AD dissolved organic P (Porg) is partially mineralized into inorganic P (Pi), which tends to associate with suspended particles (Massé et al., 2011; Möller and Müller, 2012). In the model this process has been simulated associating P mineralization to the same biodegradation rate of proteins. No losses of K forms are accounted during AD as K is considered to re- main totally in the liquid phase (Masse et al., 2005).

After animal housing, and previously to AD process, fresh slurry may be stored for a few days. As a consequence, some uncontrolled CH4 and NH3 emissions may occur and a decrease on the BVS and biogas poten- tial may be expected. This has been accounted in SIMSWASTE-AD by fol- lowing the same approach described above for the AD process, but considering the stored time at ambient conditions.

Once the AD process is finished, a common practice used on agricul- tural AD plants is to store the digestate in a tank for several weeks or months previously to be transported and applied on the field. During this period certain amounts of greenhouse gases (CH4, N20) and NH3 may be released to the atmosphere.

The magnitude of the final emissions may vary significantly depend- ing on the environmental conditions and management applied. There- fore, EFs are multiplied by variation factors (F) in order to reflect the different influence of the type and duration of storage and climate

30: A number of separation technologies are available for reducing mois- ture content in the digestate, providing a different efficiency rate of water reduction and transfer of nutrients into the dry-matter-rich fraction. In our model four treatment options have been considered: (i) decanter cen- trifuge, (ii) belt separator, (iii) screw press, (iv) sedimentation. Average separation indexes of solids and plant nutrients have been applied for every method according to reviewed literature (Hjorth et al., 2010).

However, in the case of centrifugation, several authors have pointed out that the separation efficiency is highly influenced by total solids (TS) content in the input slurry (Moller et al., 2002; Hjorth et al., 2010). Based on results of various studies (Sneath et al., 1988; Reimann, 1989; Moller et al., 2002, 2007) regression functions have been
developed in order to relate TS content and separation efficiency of the different fractions and nutrients in the digested slurry (Fig. S1).
where %TSinn is total solids of input material, and %VSF, %TSF, %TNSF and %NH4-NsF are the fractions to solid phase of input volume, total solids, total N and ammoniacal N respectively.
The SIMSWASTE-AD model accuracies and performance were evaluat- ed using regression coefficient (r2), the relative root mean square error (RMSE) and the root mean square deviation (RMSD).
31: A number of separation technologies are available for reducing mois- ture content in the digestate, providing a different efficiency rate of water reduction and transfer of nutrients into the dry-matter-rich fraction. In our model four treatment options have been considered: (i) decanter cen- trifuge, (ii) belt separator, (iii) screw press, (iv) sedimentation. Average separation indexes of solids and plant nutrients have been applied for every method according to reviewed literature (Hjorth et al., 2010).

However, in the case of centrifugation, several authors have pointed out that the separation efficiency is highly influenced by total solids (TS) content in the input slurry (Moller et al., 2002; Hjorth et al., 2010). Based on results of various studies (Sneath et al., 1988; Reimann, 1989; Moller et al., 2002, 2007) regression functions have been
developed in order to relate TS content and separation efficiency of the different fractions and nutrients in the digested slurry (Fig. S1).
where %TSinn is total solids of input material, and %VSF, %TSF, %TNSF and %NH4-NsF are the fractions to solid phase of input volume, total solids, total N and ammoniacal N respectively.
The SIMSWASTE-AD model accuracies and performance were evaluat- ed using regression coefficient (r2), the relative root mean square error (RMSE) and the root mean square deviation (RMSD).
32: A formal validation of the whole SIMSWASTE-AD model is not possible since measured whole-AD datasets are not available to "validate" all as- pects of the model. Instead, some sub-models were tested separately using available independent datasets (BMP, N mineralization and, opera- tional conditions and co-digestion). Some aspects, like post-digestion emissions, could not be validated due to the lack of available data. A final case study was applied to the whole model performance as a way to qualitative assess how well model predictions represent the actual model components.
33: Two filters were applied to select BMP datasets: (i) studies providing details of the substrate composition (protein, lipids, carbohydrates, lig- nin), (ii) datasets from assays involving substrates based on manure mix- tures (Moller et al., 2004; Amon et al., 2007; Labatut et al., 2011; Triolo et al., 2011; Ferrer et al., 2014). Model predictions agreed reasonably well with measured data (Fig. 6) as reflected by the slope close to one (0.95) and a high determination coefficient (r2 = 0.84). Root mean squared de- viation (RMSD) and root mean squared error (RMSE) were used to assess the accuracy of the model. The RMSD and RMSE from validation process was 36.7 1 CH4/kg VS and 14.0% respectively. Previous modelling studies have shown values of RMSE in the similar range (9.4-26.5%) (Amon et al., 2007; Gunaseelan, 2007, 2009; Triolo et al., 2011; Thomsen et al., 2014).

Datasets from two studies (Lehtomäki et al., 2007; Li et al., 2016) were selected for N mineralization validation as they provided enough detailed information on experimental conditions (HRT, T), substrate composition (protein, lipids, carbohydrates, lignin) and initial and final NH+- con- centration. The model predicted well the N mineralization during AD, as

Finally, the capability of SIMSWASTE-AD to capture the influence of op- erational conditions and substrate composition was evaluated. The model was tested against a dataset from Ruile et al. (2015), who inves- tigated the performance of 21 full-scale agricultural CSTR biogas plants. A variety of feedstocks are co-digested in the studied plants: namely an- imal manures (cattle, poultry, horse), energy crops (maize silage, grass silage, grain) and other organic residues (e.g. sugar beet). Since no spe- cific details of the feedstock composition were available, reference data were applied according to standard values from KTBL (2013) (Table S2). Biogas plants also varied widely in their size and key operational condi- tions: organic loading rate (OLR), retention time (HRT), process temper- ature and number of reactors (1-3 digesters).

As shown in Fig. 8, the good agreement between modelled and mea- sured values (r2 = 0.91) indicates that the model was able to simulate reasonably well the performance of the AD plants. It appears that SIMSWASTE-AD may over-predict biogas production in larger size plants, but there are insufficient points at the high end of the range to confirm this trend. Uncertainty associated with these measured data in terms of input substrate, CH4 production, and actual HRT in digesters (Ruile et al., 2015), and the use of standard values of feedstock composition in the simulation runs, must be also considered as a source of discrepancy. 40: <header>3.2. Description of scenarios for testing AD model </header>

In order to illustrate the simulated effect of region or system specific differences for AD of animal waste on emissions and final digestate composition, a range of scenarios (Table 2) were tested with SIMSWASTE-AD. We evaluated the effect of technology level (LOW_TECH, MED_TECH, HIGH_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and climate (COOL_CLIM) on GHG emissions, NH3 and nutrient flows for the treat- ment of dairy cattle manure (CM). The main differences among the sce- narios were associated with both the conditions during the AD process (temperature range, digestion stages) and also to the post-digestion stage operations, such as collecting residual biogas from digestate storaged or applying different techniques for solid-liquid separation.

The low technology (L_TECH) simulation was taken as the reference scenario. We simulated codigestion (CO_DIG) of cattle manure (CM) with maize silage (MS) on a 80:20 ratio (dry matter basis) to explore the influence of energy crops in agricultural AD plants. Default compo- sition of cattle manure and maize silage were defined according to pub- lished literature (KTBL, 2013) (Table S2).

Calculations included both direct CH4 and N20 emissions from bio- gas combustion and digestate storage and also the GHG emissions (expressed as kgCO2eq) attributed to the substitution of electricity from fossil fuels (50% coal, 46% natural gas, 4% oil) with renewable ener- gy from biogas -0.9 kgCO2eq/kWh) (Ecoinvent 3.3, 2016). Thermal energy usage was not considered but for operation of the biogas plant. In addition, we accounted for the emissions potentially avoided in a baseline scenario without AD treatment, following the IPCC methodolo- gy-Tier 2 for manure management as stored liquid slurry (IPCC, 2006).
41: <header>3.2. Description of scenarios for testing AD model </header>

In order to illustrate the simulated effect of region or system specific differences for AD of animal waste on emissions and final digestate composition, a range of scenarios (Table 2) were tested with SIMSWASTE-AD. We evaluated the effect of technology level (LOW_TECH, MED_TECH, HIGH_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and climate (COOL_CLIM) on GHG emissions, NH3 and nutrient flows for the treat- ment of dairy cattle manure (CM). The main differences among the sce- narios were associated with both the conditions during the AD process (temperature range, digestion stages) and also to the post-digestion stage operations, such as collecting residual biogas from digestate storaged or applying different techniques for solid-liquid separation.

The low technology (L_TECH) simulation was taken as the reference scenario. We simulated codigestion (CO_DIG) of cattle manure (CM) with maize silage (MS) on a 80:20 ratio (dry matter basis) to explore the influence of energy crops in agricultural AD plants. Default compo- sition of cattle manure and maize silage were defined according to pub- lished literature (KTBL, 2013) (Table S2).

Calculations included both direct CH4 and N20 emissions from bio- gas combustion and digestate storage and also the GHG emissions (expressed as kgCO2eq) attributed to the substitution of electricity from fossil fuels (50% coal, 46% natural gas, 4% oil) with renewable ener- gy from biogas -0.9 kgCO2eq/kWh) (Ecoinvent 3.3, 2016). Thermal energy usage was not considered but for operation of the biogas plant. In addition, we accounted for the emissions potentially avoided in a baseline scenario without AD treatment, following the IPCC methodolo- gy-Tier 2 for manure management as stored liquid slurry (IPCC, 2006).
42: <header>4. Results and discussion </header>
4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.
Table 2 Characteristics of the scenarios considered involving 3 technology levels for AD plants (L_TECH, M_TECH, H_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and cool climate (COOL_CLIM).

Scenario|Substrates|range|Stages|Storage|Solids separation|Climateb L_TECH|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate M_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Surface cover|Belt separator|Temperate H_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Sealed + biogas|Centrifuge|Temperate CO_DIG|CM + MS|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate COOL_CLIM|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Cool
a Cattle manure (CM), maize silage (MS). b Temperatures considered for cool (<15 °C and temperate (15-25 climate are 12 °C and 18 °C, respectively.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.
While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2

release from soil organic matter (unless no-tillage practices are applied) at the soil-cropping level, which would be accounted within the pro- posed modelling framework by linking to existing models simulating different stages of agricultural production systems (e.g. SIMSDAIRY). These losses could offset the potential GHG emissions sav- ings by the AD process (Hamelin et al., 2014). Recent studies have questioned the suitability of co-digesting crops to produce renewable energy through AD (Giuliano et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015). Energy crops cultivation involves important environmental burdens related to energy and fertilizer consumption (Hanegraaf et al., 1998). Further- more, it leads to indirect land use changes (iLUC) by fodder production displacement, which in overall terms may offset the GHG savings asso- ciated with bioenergy production (Hamelin et al., 2014; Styles et al., 2015). As an alternative co-substrate for AD, the use of agri-food waste has been recommended, since no emissions are usually assigned to the production of these resources. Nevertheless, the consequences of shifting from the current use of the agri-food waste (e.g. animal feed) must be carefully considered through a life cycle perspective, since the overall environmental benefits can be significantly affected (Tufvesson et al., 2013; Pardo et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Tonini et al., 2016).
4.2. Nutrient flows of the analysed scenarios. Influence of solid-liquid separation

Simulated concentration of ammonium N (NH-N) in the digestate increased about 30-37% in comparison to the N content in the initial feedstock. The results obtained are in the similar range of values report- ed in literature (Kirchmann and Witter, 1992; Lehtomäki and Björnsson, 2006). The processes (e.g. mineralization) that regulate the N transfor- mations in the model are mainly affected by the initial organic N con- tent, the biodegradability of the substrate and the operational conditions of the AD process (HRT, T, number of digesters). Hence,
mineralization rates were higher in those scenarios with better technol- ogy (M_TECH, H_TECH) and therefore, enhanced degradation efficiency.

In the final digestate, small but significant differences (3-6%) of total N content were observed among the manure based scenarios evaluated, which is attributed to different technology applied during the storage period. Ammonia gas represents a major pathway of N loss at this stage, especially when the digested slurry is stored in open tanks or la- goons. Therefore, mitigation strategies (e.g. surface covers) play a cru- cial role reducing these polluting emissions and enhancing N conservation.

As a final step, separation of digestate into a liquid and a solid frac- tion may be applied, in order to optimize nutrient management for crop fertilization. As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. At the same time, the production of a liquid phase with low P content, and high proportions of mineral N and K, makes it suitable for on-farm use as it meets better the nutrient (N:P:K) plant requirements, while reducing blockage risks on pipes and channels. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.
4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

SIMSWASTE-AD is part of a modelling framework that through a LCA perspective couples other models or decision support tools designed for the land application stage for example. Thereby the environmental and agronomical implications of applying digestate can be subsequently analysed. Transformations of C and N during manure management have the potential to alter N losses and N crop availability after land applica- tion. Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the

C content in the slurry, thus constraining denitrification reactions in later stages (Vallejo et al., 2006; Insam et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.

Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.

Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.

4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.
Table 2 Characteristics of the scenarios considered involving 3 technology levels for AD plants (L_TECH, M_TECH, H_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and cool climate (COOL_CLIM).

a Cattle manure (CM), maize silage (MS). b Temperatures considered for cool (<15 °C and temperate (15-25 climate are 12 °C and 18 °C, respectively.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.
While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2

release from soil organic matter (unless no-tillage practices are applied) at the soil-cropping level, which would be accounted within the pro- posed modelling framework by linking to existing models simulating different stages of agricultural production systems (e.g. SIMSDAIRY). These losses could offset the potential GHG emissions sav- ings by the AD process (Hamelin et al., 2014). Recent studies have questioned the suitability of co-digesting crops to produce renewable energy through AD (Giuliano et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015). Energy crops cultivation involves important environmental burdens related to energy and fertilizer consumption (Hanegraaf et al., 1998). Further- more, it leads to indirect land use changes (iLUC) by fodder production displacement, which in overall terms may offset the GHG savings asso- ciated with bioenergy production (Hamelin et al., 2014; Styles et al., 2015). As an alternative co-substrate for AD, the use of agri-food waste has been recommended, since no emissions are usually assigned to the production of these resources. Nevertheless, the consequences of shifting from the current use of the agri-food waste (e.g. animal feed) must be carefully considered through a life cycle perspective, since the overall environmental benefits can be significantly affected (Tufvesson et al., 2013; Pardo et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Tonini et al., 2016).
4.2. Nutrient flows of the analysed scenarios. Influence of solid-liquid separation

Simulated concentration of ammonium N (NH-N) in the digestate increased about 30-37% in comparison to the N content in the initial feedstock. The results obtained are in the similar range of values report- ed in literature (Kirchmann and Witter, 1992; Lehtomäki and Björnsson, 2006). The processes (e.g. mineralization) that regulate the N transfor- mations in the model are mainly affected by the initial organic N con- tent, the biodegradability of the substrate and the operational conditions of the AD process (HRT, T, number of digesters). Hence,
mineralization rates were higher in those scenarios with better technol- ogy (M_TECH, H_TECH) and therefore, enhanced degradation efficiency.

In the final digestate, small but significant differences (3-6%) of total N content were observed among the manure based scenarios evaluated, which is attributed to different technology applied during the storage period. Ammonia gas represents a major pathway of N loss at this stage, especially when the digested slurry is stored in open tanks or la- goons. Therefore, mitigation strategies (e.g. surface covers) play a cru- cial role reducing these polluting emissions and enhancing N conservation.

As a final step, separation of digestate into a liquid and a solid frac- tion may be applied, in order to optimize nutrient management for crop fertilization. As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. At the same time, the production of a liquid phase with low P content, and high proportions of mineral N and K, makes it suitable for on-farm use as it meets better the nutrient (N:P:K) plant requirements, while reducing blockage risks on pipes and channels. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.
4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

SIMSWASTE-AD is part of a modelling framework that through a LCA perspective couples other models or decision support tools designed for the land application stage for example. Thereby the environmental and agronomical implications of applying digestate can be subsequently analysed. Transformations of C and N during manure management have the potential to alter N losses and N crop availability after land applica- tion. Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the
