Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.
54: <header>4. Results and discussion </header>
4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.
Table 2 Characteristics of the scenarios considered involving 3 technology levels for AD plants (L_TECH, M_TECH, H_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and cool climate (COOL_CLIM).

Scenario|Substrates|range|Stages|Storage|Solids separation|Climateb L_TECH|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate M_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Surface cover|Belt separator|Temperate H_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Sealed + biogas|Centrifuge|Temperate CO_DIG|CM + MS|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate COOL_CLIM|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Cool
a Cattle manure (CM), maize silage (MS). b Temperatures considered for cool (<15 °C and temperate (15-25 climate are 12 °C and 18 °C, respectively.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.
While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2

release from soil organic matter (unless no-tillage practices are applied) at the soil-cropping level, which would be accounted within the pro- posed modelling framework by linking to existing models simulating different stages of agricultural production systems (e.g. SIMSDAIRY). These losses could offset the potential GHG emissions sav- ings by the AD process (Hamelin et al., 2014). Recent studies have questioned the suitability of co-digesting crops to produce renewable energy through AD (Giuliano et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015). Energy crops cultivation involves important environmental burdens related to energy and fertilizer consumption (Hanegraaf et al., 1998). Further- more, it leads to indirect land use changes (iLUC) by fodder production displacement, which in overall terms may offset the GHG savings asso- ciated with bioenergy production (Hamelin et al., 2014; Styles et al., 2015). As an alternative co-substrate for AD, the use of agri-food waste has been recommended, since no emissions are usually assigned to the production of these resources. Nevertheless, the consequences of shifting from the current use of the agri-food waste (e.g. animal feed) must be carefully considered through a life cycle perspective, since the overall environmental benefits can be significantly affected (Tufvesson et al., 2013; Pardo et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Tonini et al., 2016).
4.2. Nutrient flows of the analysed scenarios. Influence of solid-liquid separation

Simulated concentration of ammonium N (NH-N) in the digestate increased about 30-37% in comparison to the N content in the initial feedstock. The results obtained are in the similar range of values report- ed in literature (Kirchmann and Witter, 1992; Lehtomäki and Björnsson, 2006). The processes (e.g. mineralization) that regulate the N transfor- mations in the model are mainly affected by the initial organic N con- tent, the biodegradability of the substrate and the operational conditions of the AD process (HRT, T, number of digesters). Hence,
mineralization rates were higher in those scenarios with better technol- ogy (M_TECH, H_TECH) and therefore, enhanced degradation efficiency.

In the final digestate, small but significant differences (3-6%) of total N content were observed among the manure based scenarios evaluated, which is attributed to different technology applied during the storage period. Ammonia gas represents a major pathway of N loss at this stage, especially when the digested slurry is stored in open tanks or la- goons. Therefore, mitigation strategies (e.g. surface covers) play a cru- cial role reducing these polluting emissions and enhancing N conservation.

As a final step, separation of digestate into a liquid and a solid frac- tion may be applied, in order to optimize nutrient management for crop fertilization. As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. At the same time, the production of a liquid phase with low P content, and high proportions of mineral N and K, makes it suitable for on-farm use as it meets better the nutrient (N:P:K) plant requirements, while reducing blockage risks on pipes and channels. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.
4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

SIMSWASTE-AD is part of a modelling framework that through a LCA perspective couples other models or decision support tools designed for the land application stage for example. Thereby the environmental and agronomical implications of applying digestate can be subsequently analysed. Transformations of C and N during manure management have the potential to alter N losses and N crop availability after land applica- tion. Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the

C content in the slurry, thus constraining denitrification reactions in later stages (Vallejo et al., 2006; Insam et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.

As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. At the same time, the production of a liquid phase with low P content, and high proportions of mineral N and K, makes it suitable for on-farm use as it meets better the nutrient (N:P:K) plant requirements, while reducing blockage risks on pipes and channels. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.
4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

SIMSWASTE-AD is part of a modelling framework that through a LCA perspective couples other models or decision support tools designed for the land application stage for example. Thereby the environmental and agronomical implications of applying digestate can be subsequently analysed. Transformations of C and N during manure management have the potential to alter N losses and N crop availability after land applica- tion. Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the
Table 4

Model|Functionalities|Parameters organic fertilizer|References DAYCENT|SOC and N dynamics|C:N, C:P, Lignin, NH4-N NO3 N°|Parton et al., 1998; Del Grosso et al., 2001 DNDC|SOC and N dynamics|P, NH4 -N°, NO3 N°|Li et al., 1992; Li, 2000 FASSET|SOC and N dynamics|DM, Norge Namm|Jacobsen et al., 1998 RothC|SOC dynamics|C (DPM:RPM:HUM)|Coleman and Jenkinson, 1996 MANNER|N dynamics|TN, NH4 NO3 N, DM|Chambers et al., 2006 NGAUGE|N dynamics|DM, Norg, Ninorg|Brown et al., 2005

C content in the slurry, thus constraining denitrification reactions in later stages (Vallejo et al., 2006; Insam et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.

4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.
Table 2 Characteristics of the scenarios considered involving 3 technology levels for AD plants (L_TECH, M_TECH, H_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and cool climate (COOL_CLIM).

Scenario|Substrates|range|Stages|Storage|Solids separation|Climateb L_TECH|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate M_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Surface cover|Belt separator|Temperate H_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Sealed + biogas|Centrifuge|Temperate CO_DIG|CM + MS|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate COOL_CLIM|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Cool
a Cattle manure (CM), maize silage (MS). b Temperatures considered for cool (<15 °C and temperate (15-25 climate are 12 °C and 18 °C, respectively.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.
While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2

release from soil organic matter (unless no-tillage practices are applied) at the soil-cropping level, which would be accounted within the pro- posed modelling framework by linking to existing models simulating different stages of agricultural production systems (e.g. SIMSDAIRY). These losses could offset the potential GHG emissions sav- ings by the AD process (Hamelin et al., 2014). Recent studies have questioned the suitability of co-digesting crops to produce renewable energy through AD (Giuliano et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015). Energy crops cultivation involves important environmental burdens related to energy and fertilizer consumption (Hanegraaf et al., 1998). Further- more, it leads to indirect land use changes (iLUC) by fodder production displacement, which in overall terms may offset the GHG savings asso- ciated with bioenergy production (Hamelin et al., 2014; Styles et al., 2015). As an alternative co-substrate for AD, the use of agri-food waste has been recommended, since no emissions are usually assigned to the production of these resources. Nevertheless, the consequences of shifting from the current use of the agri-food waste (e.g. animal feed) must be carefully considered through a life cycle perspective, since the overall environmental benefits can be significantly affected (Tufvesson et al., 2013; Pardo et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Tonini et al., 2016).
4.2. Nutrient flows of the analysed scenarios. Influence of solid-liquid separation

Simulated concentration of ammonium N (NH-N) in the digestate increased about 30-37% in comparison to the N content in the initial feedstock. The results obtained are in the similar range of values report- ed in literature (Kirchmann and Witter, 1992; Lehtomäki and Björnsson, 2006). The processes (e.g. mineralization) that regulate the N transfor- mations in the model are mainly affected by the initial organic N con- tent, the biodegradability of the substrate and the operational conditions of the AD process (HRT, T, number of digesters). Hence,
mineralization rates were higher in those scenarios with better technol- ogy (M_TECH, H_TECH) and therefore, enhanced degradation efficiency.

In the final digestate, small but significant differences (3-6%) of total N content were observed among the manure based scenarios evaluated, which is attributed to different technology applied during the storage period. Ammonia gas represents a major pathway of N loss at this stage, especially when the digested slurry is stored in open tanks or la- goons. Therefore, mitigation strategies (e.g. surface covers) play a cru- cial role reducing these polluting emissions and enhancing N conservation.

As a final step, separation of digestate into a liquid and a solid frac- tion may be applied, in order to optimize nutrient management for crop fertilization. As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.

While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2

release from soil organic matter (unless no-tillage practices are applied) at the soil-cropping level, which would be accounted within the pro- posed modelling framework by linking to existing models simulating different stages of agricultural production systems (e.g. SIMSDAIRY). These losses could offset the potential GHG emissions sav- ings by the AD process (Hamelin et al., 2014). Recent studies have questioned the suitability of co-digesting crops to produce renewable energy through AD (Giuliano et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015). Energy crops cultivation involves important environmental burdens related to energy and fertilizer consumption (Hanegraaf et al., 1998). Further- more, it leads to indirect land use changes (iLUC) by fodder production displacement, which in overall terms may offset the GHG savings asso- ciated with bioenergy production (Hamelin et al., 2014; Styles et al., 2015). As an alternative co-substrate for AD, the use of agri-food waste has been recommended, since no emissions are usually assigned to the production of these resources. Nevertheless, the consequences of shifting from the current use of the agri-food waste (e.g. animal feed) must be carefully considered through a life cycle perspective, since the overall environmental benefits can be significantly affected (Tufvesson et al., 2013; Pardo et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Tonini et al., 2016).
4.2. Nutrient flows of the analysed scenarios. Influence of solid-liquid separation

Simulated concentration of ammonium N (NH-N) in the digestate increased about 30-37% in comparison to the N content in the initial feedstock. The results obtained are in the similar range of values report- ed in literature (Kirchmann and Witter, 1992; Lehtomäki and Björnsson, 2006). The processes (e.g. mineralization) that regulate the N transfor- mations in the model are mainly affected by the initial organic N con- tent, the biodegradability of the substrate and the operational conditions of the AD process (HRT, T, number of digesters). Hence,
mineralization rates were higher in those scenarios with better technol- ogy (M_TECH, H_TECH) and therefore, enhanced degradation efficiency.

In the final digestate, small but significant differences (3-6%) of total N content were observed among the manure based scenarios evaluated, which is attributed to different technology applied during the storage period. Ammonia gas represents a major pathway of N loss at this stage, especially when the digested slurry is stored in open tanks or la- goons. Therefore, mitigation strategies (e.g. surface covers) play a cru- cial role reducing these polluting emissions and enhancing N conservation.

As a final step, separation of digestate into a liquid and a solid frac- tion may be applied, in order to optimize nutrient management for crop fertilization. As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. At the same time, the production of a liquid phase with low P content, and high proportions of mineral N and K, makes it suitable for on-farm use as it meets better the nutrient (N:P:K) plant requirements, while reducing blockage risks on pipes and channels. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.
4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

SIMSWASTE-AD is part of a modelling framework that through a LCA perspective couples other models or decision support tools designed for the land application stage for example. Thereby the environmental and agronomical implications of applying digestate can be subsequently analysed. Transformations of C and N during manure management have the potential to alter N losses and N crop availability after land applica- tion. Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the

C content in the slurry, thus constraining denitrification reactions in later stages (Vallejo et al., 2006; Insam et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.
58: <header>4. Results and discussion </header>
4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.
Table 2 Characteristics of the scenarios considered involving 3 technology levels for AD plants (L_TECH, M_TECH, H_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and cool climate (COOL_CLIM).

a Cattle manure (CM), maize silage (MS). b Temperatures considered for cool (<15 °C and temperate (15-25 climate are 12 °C and 18 °C, respectively.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.
While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2

59: <header>4. Results and discussion </header>
4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.

release from soil organic matter (unless no-tillage practices are applied) at the soil-cropping level, which would be accounted within the pro- posed modelling framework by linking to existing models simulating different stages of agricultural production systems (e.g. SIMSDAIRY). These losses could offset the potential GHG emissions sav- ings by the AD process (Hamelin et al., 2014). Recent studies have questioned the suitability of co-digesting crops to produce renewable energy through AD (Giuliano et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015). Energy crops cultivation involves important environmental burdens related to energy and fertilizer consumption (Hanegraaf et al., 1998). Further- more, it leads to indirect land use changes (iLUC) by fodder production displacement, which in overall terms may offset the GHG savings asso- ciated with bioenergy production (Hamelin et al., 2014; Styles et al., 2015). As an alternative co-substrate for AD, the use of agri-food waste has been recommended, since no emissions are usually assigned to the production of these resources. Nevertheless, the consequences of shifting from the current use of the agri-food waste (e.g. animal feed) must be carefully considered through a life cycle perspective, since the overall environmental benefits can be significantly affected (Tufvesson et al., 2013; Pardo et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2016; Tonini et al., 2016).
4.2. Nutrient flows of the analysed scenarios. Influence of solid-liquid separation

Simulated concentration of ammonium N (NH-N) in the digestate increased about 30-37% in comparison to the N content in the initial feedstock. The results obtained are in the similar range of values report- ed in literature (Kirchmann and Witter, 1992; Lehtomäki and Björnsson, 2006). The processes (e.g. mineralization) that regulate the N transfor- mations in the model are mainly affected by the initial organic N con- tent, the biodegradability of the substrate and the operational conditions of the AD process (HRT, T, number of digesters). Hence,
mineralization rates were higher in those scenarios with better technol- ogy (M_TECH, H_TECH) and therefore, enhanced degradation efficiency.

In the final digestate, small but significant differences (3-6%) of total N content were observed among the manure based scenarios evaluated, which is attributed to different technology applied during the storage period. Ammonia gas represents a major pathway of N loss at this stage, especially when the digested slurry is stored in open tanks or la- goons. Therefore, mitigation strategies (e.g. surface covers) play a cru- cial role reducing these polluting emissions and enhancing N conservation.

As a final step, separation of digestate into a liquid and a solid frac- tion may be applied, in order to optimize nutrient management for crop fertilization. As observed in the results from the H_TECH scenario (Table 4), decanter centrifuge is the most efficient technique for retaining P - and to some extent N- in a DM-rich fraction. This facilitates the transport, and therefore export of nutrients (N,P) to cropland areas with high fertilizer demand. At the same time, the production of a liquid phase with low P content, and high proportions of mineral N and K, makes it suitable for on-farm use as it meets better the nutrient (N:P:K) plant requirements, while reducing blockage risks on pipes and channels. Filtration technologies, such as a belt separator (M_TECH), can also achieve a solid phase with high DM content (Table 4). However, they are less efficient on P removal, which can make a crucial difference in terms of nutrient management.

Despite not being selected in the defined scenarios; it is worth to mention that sedimentation is also included in the model as an available technique for solid-liquid separation. Sedimentation is considered to be an efficient method to produce a solid fraction with high P, and relative- ly high N content at low operation costs. Other aspects, such as invest- ment costs, and retention time requirements must be carefully analysed, but they are out of the scope of this analysis.
4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

SIMSWASTE-AD is part of a modelling framework that through a LCA perspective couples other models or decision support tools designed for the land application stage for example. Thereby the environmental and agronomical implications of applying digestate can be subsequently analysed. Transformations of C and N during manure management have the potential to alter N losses and N crop availability after land applica- tion. Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the

C content in the slurry, thus constraining denitrification reactions in later stages (Vallejo et al., 2006; Insam et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.

4.3. Effects of AD on downstream stages. Environmental and agronomical implications through modelling approaches

Among the AD scenarios tested, an increase in NH4 content up to 15% is estimated from L_TECH to H_TECH. The application of better technologies (H_TECH) improves digestate fertilizer potential, but it may also increase the risk of NH3-N losses through volatilization when it is applied in the field. In order to examine these effects soil-plant models represent a very helpful tool, but they must be able to reflect the fraction of ammonium N in the organic fertilizer (Table 5).

Furthermore, soil initrification-denitrification (N-DN) processes that lead to N20 emissions are also influenced by the composition of the added slurry, and therefore by previous AD treatment. A decrease in N20 emissions can be expected after digestate application from H_TECH scenario in comparison to low treated digestate (L-TECH). In- creased biodegradation during AD results in a higher reduction of the

C content in the slurry, thus constraining denitrification reactions in later stages (Vallejo et al., 2006; Insam et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the final effect on N2/N20 emissions will be determined by the complex in- teractions involved in the soil-plant system, which are highly controlled by the environmental conditions (water content, T) and soil character- istics (Thomsen et al., 2010; Möller and Müller, 2012; Möller, 2015).

Changes in the soil organic C (SOC) dynamics can occur (Thomsen et al., 2013; Möller, 2015), which can be explored through biogeochemical models. The production of biogas (CO2, CH4) involves a fraction of C that is released to the atmosphere, instead of being applied to the land. In our study the most efficient AD process (H_TECH) results in a decrease of 7% of VS (i.e. C) available in the digestate when compared to L TECH (Table 4).
4.4. Comparison with other AD models. Applicability, limitations and strength of the SIMSWASTE-AD model

Effective evaluation of agricultural mitigation strategies must be assessed from a system level approach in order to fully account for po- tential effects on other agricultural stages and affected systems; and to evaluate the risks of pollution swapping by considering multiple envi- ronmental impact categories (Schils et al., 2005; Del Prado et al., 2013; Petersen et al., 2013; Van Middelaar et al., 2013). With the aim to strengthen this aspect, SIMSWASTE-AD provides not only the biogas and energy production associated with a specific AD treatment but also the C and nutrient (N, P, K) flows and the main emissions. Hence, al- though primarily designed to be applied within the SIMSDAIRY modelling framework, as IMSWASTE-AD is based on a mass balance approach, it can be integrated with other existing farm models simulating flows and losses of C and N (Olesen et al., 2006; Schils et al., 2007) and LCA ap- proaches (Cederberg et al., 2013; Styles et al., 2015).

While simple approaches for CH4 prediction (stoichiometric models, regression models) lack of flexibility to capture management conditions effects; complex mechanistic AD models (e.g. AMD1) rely on a large number of equations demanding detailed parametrization, which makes difficult their widespread implementation. In an attempt to pro- vide farmers and other stakeholders with a decision support tool for promoting AD, a number of simple biogas calculators have been devel- oped in the last years (e.g. BEAT, FarmWare). Most of the available cal- culators are based on very simple methodologies to estimate the biogas and energy production from a specific waste stream. Less commonly en- vironment-related outputs, such as GHG mitigation potential, are esti- mated, while the influence of operational and climatic conditions on the AD mass balance are rarely captured (Kythreotou et al., 2014).

SIMSWASTE-AD aims to help on the decision making process of many environmental strategies at farm or regional scale, where AD is involved and which are strongly linked to the specific management practices at farm level. The ultimate efficacy of these measures often relies on farm decisions on production-related activities (e.g. technology level, feeding, fertilization, soil preparation), which interact with other biophysical fac- tors (e.g. climate and soil conditions) (Paloma et al., 2013). For example, recent studies have explored a measure to reduce GHG emissions at the animal level by increasing fat intake in the diet of cows (Moller et al., 2014). They observed a reduction in enteric CH4 emissions, but also a shift in the faeces composition which leads to an increase in the CH4

production potential. This effect may lead to higher CH4 emissions dur- ing manure storage, thus partially counteracting the GHG emissions saved in the previous stage. However, if AD is applied, GHG mitigation effect can be increased through the enhanced CH4 production potential from the manure. The overall effects and consequences of these kinds of strategies could be explored with SIMSWASTE-AD when implemented within an integrated modelling framework.

4.1. Gaseous emissions (CH4, N20, NH3) of the analysed scenarios

The results from the simulation runs are displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 9. In all the evaluated scenarios AD treatment achieve GHG savings due to both energy production from biogas substituting fossil fuels and avoided emissions compared with the baseline manure management considered (conventional slurry storage). Estimates of avoided GHG emissions should be carefully interpreted, as they may vary depending on the accounting method and assumptions considered with regards to avoided processes (e.g. energy production, baseline manure manage- ment). However the general trend towards achieving GHG savings through manure AD is very clear in this case.
Table 2 Characteristics of the scenarios considered involving 3 technology levels for AD plants (L_TECH, M_TECH, H_TECH), co-digestion (CO_DIG) and cool climate (COOL_CLIM).

Scenario|Substrates|range|Stages|Storage|Solids separation|Climateb L_TECH|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate M_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Surface cover|Belt separator|Temperate H_TECH|CM|Thermophilic|2|Sealed + biogas|Centrifuge|Temperate CO_DIG|CM + MS|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Temperate COOL_CLIM|CM|Mesophilic|1|Open|No separation|Cool
a Cattle manure (CM), maize silage (MS). b Temperatures considered for cool (<15 °C and temperate (15-25 climate are 12 °C and 18 °C, respectively.

Scenarios with improved technology (M_TECH, H_TECH) showed a clear reduction in overall GHG emissions (up to 36%). This is mainly at- tributed to higher biogas yields achieved due to higher HRT (two- stages) and T range (thermophilic), but also to the emissions avoided from the post-digestion stage when prevention practices are applied in the digestate storage tank.

Reactor technological advances, such as thermophilic range and number of reactors have an influence on other important aspects which are not captured by the model. A proper sanitation of the digestate is of major importance for the reuse as a fertilizer on agricul- tural land (van Lier et al., 2001). This can only be ensured by thermo- philic conditions or through a specific hygienization stage before or after the mesophilic reactor. In addition, the use of two digesters, con- nected in series, prevent the risk of short circuits, which may result in a loss of untreated biomass to the storage tank involving sanitary risks and more CH4 emissions to the atmosphere (Angelidaki et al., 2006; Kaparaju et al., 2009).

Technology used also affected substantially the gaseous emissions during the post-digestion stage. In the M_TECH scenario, reductions of CH4 and NH3 of 50% are shown when a surface cover is applied in the digestate storage tank (Fig. 9). Nevertheless, the most effective mitiga- tion measure at this stage is to seal the storage tank with a gas tight cover (H_TECH). As a result, an overall GHG decrease of 36% is achieved due to a drastic reduction of direct emissions from digestate and an ad- ditional bioenergy production, which enhances the economic and envi- ronmental benefits of the AD process (Clemens et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2007).

The importance of CH4 post-digestion emissions becomes even clearer when analysing the results from the scenarios where the effects of co-digestion and cool climate are tested (CODIG, COOL_CLIM) (Table 3) (Fig.9). In the CODIG scenario, adding maize silage (with a high bio- gas potential) to dairy manure, results in an increase of biogas produc- tion of about 23% (in terms of g/m3, which is in the same order of the values reported in literature (Lehtomäki et al., 2007). In the post-diges- tion stage, however, higher GHG emissions (25%) than the reference scenario, are shown, which are similar with the results from full scale studies (Lindorfer et al., 2008). Maize silage provides an additional source of C, which is partially converted into biogas during AD.

However, if operational conditions (HRT, T) do not guarantee a high degradation level in the digester, the remaining fraction of C in the digestate can be enriched. In these circumstances (CODIG scenario), the increase in post-digestion CH4 emissions can offset most of the GHG avoided attributed to additional energy production from co-diges- tion with crops (Fig. 9).

Climatic conditions can have an important effect on the potential GHG savings from AD, as CH4 and NH3 emissions are highly correlated with temperature. In our analysis, when a cool climate (COOL_CLIM) is simulated (12 °C the post-digestion emissions of CH4 and NH3 are re- duced 73% and 38% respectively. A similar effect has been observed in several studies (Külling et al., 2002; Sommer et al., 2000; Clemens et al., 2006). However, gaseous losses from baseline manure management (i.e. slurry storage) also decrease in colder conditions, thus reducing partially the overall GHG savings from the AD process. According to this, manure treatment through AD should be especially promoted in warmer regions for GHG mitigation purposes, as the potential for GHGs reduction is higher in these areas.

Controlling post-digestion emissions (Amon et al., 2006b) and its re- lationship with the AD performance and substrate composition have been stressed by several authors as key issues in order to reduce the net GHG emissions from the whole AD treatment (Clemens et al., 2006; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Ruile et al., 2015). The collection of biogas from covered storage tanks is highly rec- ommended, as well as those options that can improve the reactor effi- ciency (e.g. longer HRTs). Especially, post-digestion emissions can be very relevant for temperate and warm climates. In these cases, open storage tanks for digestate must be avoided. In contrast, if the storage tank is sealed and a gas tight cover is used to collect the residual biogas, warm temperatures may be desirable as they enhance methanogen ac- tivity and therefore, CH4 production.

The highest reduction in overall simulated GHG emissions is ob- served in the H_TECH scenario, when the residual biogas is collected from the storage tank. However, the overall GHG reduction could pre- sumably be even higher if co-digestion with maize silage was applied in similar technology conditions.
While improving the environmental performance from the AD pro- cess, the use of maize silage could involve large N emissions and CO2
